techniques.name: Manipulating State
techniques.description:
<xhtml:p>The adversary modifies state information maintained by the target software or causes a state transition in hardware. If successful, the target will use this tainted state and execute in an unintended manner.</xhtml:p>
            <xhtml:p>State management is an important functi

techniques.name: Manipulating Writeable Configuration Files
techniques.description: Generally these are manually edited files that are not in the preview of the system administrators, any ability on the attackers' behalf to modify these files, for example in a CVS repository, gives unauthorized access directly to the application, the same as authorized users.

techniques.name: Manipulating Web Input to File System Calls
techniques.description: An attacker manipulates inputs to the target software which the target software passes to file system calls in the OS. The goal is to gain access to, and perhaps modify, areas of the file system that the target software did not intend to be accessible.

techniques.name: Manipulating User-Controlled Variables
techniques.description: This attack targets user controlled variables (DEBUG=1, PHP Globals, and So Forth). techniques.description: This attack targets the use of the backslash in alternate encoding. techniques.name: Using Slashes in Alternate Encoding
techniques.description: This attack targets the encoding of the Slash characters. techniques.name: Web Server Logs Tampering

techniques.name: Buffer Overflow in Local Command-Line Utilities
techniques.description: This attack targets command-line utilities available in a number of shells. An adversary can leverage a vulnerability found in a command-line utility to escalate privilege to root.

techniques.description: This attack targets the log files of the target host. techniques.name: Adversary in the Middle (AiTM)
techniques.description: <xhtml:p>An adversary targets the communication between two components (typically client and server), in order to alter or obtain data from transactions. A general approach entails the adversary placing themself within the communication channel between the two components.</xhtml:p>

techniques.name: WSDL Scanning

techniques.name: Phishing

techniques.description: This attack pattern has been deprecated as it a generalization of CAPEC-230: XML Nested Payloads and CAPEC-231: XML Oversized Payloads. Please refer to these CAPECs going forward.

techniques.name: Command-Line Interface
techniques.description: Adversaries may utilize command-line interfaces (CLIs) to interact with systems and execute commands. techniques.name: Drive-by Compromise
techniques.description:
Adversaries may gain access to a system during a drive-by compromise, when a user visits a website as part of a regular browsing session. With this technique, the user's web browser is targeted and exploited simply by visiting the compromised website.

techniques.name: Exploitation for Evasion
techniques.description:

Adversaries may modify the tasking of a controller to allow for the execution of their own programs. This can allow an adversary to manipulate the execution flow and behavior of a controller.

techniques.name: Loss of View

techniques.description: Adversaries may modify parameters used to instruct industrial control system devices. These devices operate via programs that dictate how and when to perform actions based on such parameters. Such parameters can determine the extent to which an action is performed and may specify additional options.

techniques.name: Loss of Protection

techniques.name: Lateral Tool Transfer
techniques.description: Adversaries may transfer tools or other files from one system to another to stage adversary tools or other files over the course of an operation. techniques.name: Detect Operating Mode
techniques.description: Adversaries may gather information about a PLCs or controllers current operating mode. techniques.name: Execution through API
techniques.description: Adversaries may attempt to leverage Application Program Interfaces (APIs) used for communication between control software and the hardware. Specific functionality is often coded into APIs which can be called by software to engage specific functions on a device or other software.

techniques.name: Indicator Removal on Host

techniques.name: Hooking
